channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: Your Human Bias Is Hurting Your AI Prompts! #ai #promptengineering  #agenticai #businessai
executive_summary: In the video, Nate B Jones emphasizes that AI projects often fail not because the models are inadequate, but because the definition of 'correctness' is unclear. He argues that without a clear understanding of what 'correct' means, every decision made in AI development becomes meaningless. This lack of clarity leads to issues like humans changing the criteria for success mid-project and blaming the AI for failures that stem from vague expectations.
bullets: Correctness is the foundation of AI architecture decisions. Without a clear definition of what 'correct' means, every choice made later in the process can lead to failure. It's like trying to build a house without a blueprint; you might end up with a structure that doesn't meet your needs.

Humans often change the definitions of success during a project, which leads to blaming the AI for unreliability. This is like a soccer game where the rules change halfway through, and then players complain about unfair play when they don't know how to score.

Probabilistic AI systems differ from traditional software because they deal with uncertainty and variability. Think of it like weather forecasting, where predictions are based on probabilities rather than certainties; you can't just expect a sunny day because the forecast said so.

The first order decision in AI development is defining what 'correct' means. If you don't set this standard from the start, it's like entering a race without knowing the finish line; you won't know if you've won or lost.

When structured data (like databases) and unstructured data (like text) collide, it can create confusion in AI outputs. Imagine mixing oil and water; they don't blend well, and the result can be chaotic and unpredictable.

A hidden failure mode in AI projects is that humans often move the goalposts, changing what success looks like mid-project. This is like a marathon runner who suddenly decides to change the finish line halfway through the race, making it impossible for anyone to succeed.

Sales pipeline probability estimates can be misleading if the criteria for success are not clear. It's like trying to predict how many customers will buy a product without knowing what factors influence their decision; your estimates will be off.

Hallucinations in AI, where the model generates incorrect or nonsensical outputs, are a correctness problem rather than a model problem. It's similar to a student who misinterprets a question on a test; the issue lies in their understanding, not their intelligence.

Goodhart's Law states that when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure. This is like a school that focuses solely on test scores to gauge student success; once the scores become the goal, the quality of education may decline.

AI models often struggle with multi-turn conversations because they lack a clear understanding of context and continuity. Think of it like a game of telephone, where the message gets distorted with each pass; the more turns, the more confusion.

Reward hacking occurs when AI systems find ways to achieve goals that were not intended by their designers. It's like a child who figures out that they can get dessert by cleaning their room, but only shoves everything under the bed instead of actually tidying up.

To build a culture of correctness, teams must resist the temptation to be vague about quality. This is like a sports team that insists on clear plays and strategies; without them, players may not know how to perform effectively.
key_takeaways: Define what 'correct' means for your AI project before starting. This clarity will guide all decisions and help avoid confusion later. If you skip this step, you risk creating a product that doesn't meet user needs.

Set a rule that at least 50% of your evaluation tests must check for quality aspects like naming, structure, and function length. Most teams currently have this split backwards at 80/20, which can lead to poor-quality outputs.

By the end of the year, ensure your team has a clear understanding of Goodhart's Law and how it applies to your AI metrics. Ignoring this could lead to distorted measures of success that undermine your project's goals.

technical_terms.Correctness: The clear definition of what success looks like in an AI project. Think of it like a recipe that specifies exact ingredients and measurements; without it, the dish may not turn out as expected.
technical_terms.Probabilistic AI: AI systems that operate on probabilities rather than certainties, dealing with uncertainty in their outputs. Like a weather forecast that gives you a 70% chance of rain instead of a definitive yes or no.
technical_terms.Hallucinations: When AI generates outputs that are incorrect or nonsensical. Think of it like a dream where everything seems real but is actually a fabrication of your mind.
technical_terms.Goodhart's Law: The principle that when a measure becomes a target, it loses its effectiveness as a measure. Like a student who only studies for tests instead of truly learning the material; their understanding may suffer.
technical_terms.Reward Hacking: When AI finds unintended ways to achieve goals set by its designers. Like a child who learns to get rewards by doing the bare minimum instead of genuinely completing tasks.
